definition: k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN) is a non-parametric, instance-based supervised learner: at prediction time it finds the k training points closest to a query x (by a chosen distance metric), then classifies by majority label vote or regresses by averaging neighbor targets. There is no separate training phase beyond storing labeled data. This topic is the canonical home for the k-NN algorithm, the role of k, and majority-vote / neighbor-average prediction rules.
layman: k-NN answers "what do similar past examples look like?" To classify a new point, find the k closest labeled examples in your training set and take the most common label among them. For numbers, average their values instead. You pick k — small k follows local quirks; large k smooths out noise. Distance and feature scaling matter a lot.
content:
## k-Nearest Neighbors

**k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN)** is a **non-parametric**, **instance-based** (lazy) supervised algorithm. It does not learn a fixed function **w** during a training phase. Instead it **memorizes** the training set **{(xᵢ, yᵢ)}** and, for a new query **x**, decides by **local similarity**.

This topic is the **canonical home** for the **k-NN algorithm**, **choice of k**, and **majority vote / neighbor average** prediction. **Distance formulas** (Euclidean, Manhattan, Minkowski, cosine) live in **Distance Metrics** and **Norms and Distances**; **feature scaling** in **Scaling and Normalization**; **bias–variance effects of k** in **Model Complexity**.

### Prediction rule (classification)

1. Compute distance from **x** to every training point **xᵢ** (see linked metric topics for definitions).
2. Select the **k** training indices with **smallest** distance.
3. **Majority vote:** predicted class **ŷ** is the label that appears most often among those **k** neighbors (break ties by smallest distance to **x**, random tie-break, or weighted vote).

**Example:** **k = 5**, neighbor labels `{A, A, B, A, B}` → predict **A** (3 vs 2).

### Prediction rule (regression)

Same neighbor set; predict **ŷ = (1/k) Σ yⱼ** over neighbor targets **yⱼ**. **Weighted k-NN** uses weights **wⱼ ∝ 1 / d(x, xⱼ)** (or **1 / d²**) so closer neighbors count more — still k-NN, with a different aggregation rule.

### Role of hyperparameter k

- **Small k (e.g. k = 1):** decision boundary is very flexible, fits local noise → **low bias, high variance** (see **Model Complexity**).
- **Large k:** predictions average over a wider region → smoother boundary, **higher bias, lower variance**.
- **k = 1** is the 1-NN rule; **k = n** (all points) collapses to the global majority class or mean target (constant predictor).

**Rule of thumb:** start with **k = ⌊√n⌋** for classification on **n** training points, then tune on validation data. Use **odd k** for binary classification to reduce tie votes.

### Lazy learning vs eager learning

| Aspect | k-NN | Linear models, trees, SVM |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Training | Store **(X, y)** | Fit parameters from data |
| Inference | Scan (or index) all training points | Fixed formula on **x** |
| Memory | Grows with training set | Usually fixed model size |

k-NN is **lazy**: work moves to **query time**.

### Distance and preprocessing (pointers)

- Default in many libraries: **Euclidean distance** on feature vectors. Formal definitions: **Norms and Distances** (L₂, L₁, etc.) and **Distance Metrics** (when to use which for k-NN).
- **Unscaled features** dominate distance when one column has large numeric range (e.g. income vs age). **Standardize or normalize** before k-NN — see **Scaling and Normalization**.
- In **high dimensions**, all points can become roughly equidistant (**curse of dimensionality**); k-NN degrades unless dimensionality is reduced or features are informative.

### Complexity (informal)

- **Naive prediction:** **O(n · d)** per query — **n** training points, **d** features — compare **x** to every stored vector.
- **Training:** **O(1)** aside from storing data (no gradient, no tree build).
- **Speed-ups:** **k-d trees**, **ball trees**, **LSH** for low-to-moderate **d**; exact k-NN at scale often uses specialized libraries or approximate NN search.

### Decision boundary intuition

k-NN induces a **Voronoi-like** partition when **k = 1**: each training point owns the region closest to it. For **k > 1**, boundaries are **piecewise smooth** curves/surfaces formed by equidistant sets of **k** points — highly flexible compared to a single linear hyperplane.

### When k-NN fits the problem

- **Moderate n, low-to-moderate d**, labels/targets vary smoothly in feature space.
- **Nonlinear** class boundaries without hand-built features.
- **Multi-class** classification with simple majority vote.

Poor fit when **n** or **d** is huge (slow queries, sparse distances), **real-time inference** on edge devices with tight latency, or when a **compact interpretable model** is required.

### Worked toy example (classification)

2D points, **k = 3**. Query **x = (2.1, 1.0)**. Three nearest training neighbors have labels **{cat, cat, dog}** → predict **cat**.

If the third neighbor were **dog** and distances tied, use the neighbor with smallest distance to **x** or a weighted vote.

### Summary map

| Concept | Where |
| --- | --- |
| **k-NN algorithm & k** | **This topic** (canonical) |
| **Euclidean / L₁ / metric choice** | **Distance Metrics**, **Norms and Distances** |
| **Feature scaling before distance** | **Scaling and Normalization** |
| **Effect of k on bias/variance** | **Model Complexity** |
| **Margin-based boundary (contrast)** | **Support Vector Machines** |
keyPoints: k-NN stores training data and predicts from the k closest neighbors by a distance metric — no parametric training phase.
Classification: majority label among k neighbors; regression: average (or weighted average) of neighbor targets.
Small k → flexible, high-variance boundaries; large k → smoother, higher-bias predictions — tune k on validation data.
Scale features before computing distances; metric definitions live in Distance Metrics and Norms and Distances.
Naive query cost is O(n·d) per point; use spatial indexes or approximate NN when n or latency constraints grow.
